text: robotic-assisted excision of type I choledochal cyst with hepatico-duodenostomy. A fifteen-year-old female with BMI of forty-five and no other significant medical history underwent workup for recurrent right upper quadrant abdominal pain. Ultrasonography detected extrahepatic cystic lesion. Subsequent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography demonstrated a three point seven by three point zero centimeter type one choledochal cyst. Thus, she underwent robotic assisted excision of choledochal cysts with hepatico-duodenostomy.

The surgeon places a 12mm umbilical trocar, followed by three 8mm robotic trocars: one below the left costal margin in the midclavicular line, one at the umbilicus in the right midclavicular line, and one in the right flank cephalad to the second trocar. A 5mm step trocar is positioned between the first robotic trocar and the umbilical trocar as an assistant port.

The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to divide extensive adhesions between the omentum and gallbladder. The gallbladder infundibulum and cystic duct are traced to the choledochal cyst. The cystic duct is clipped and divided with robotic endoshears. Through retraction and meticulous blunt and electrocautery dissection, the choledochal cyst is circumferentially freed from adhesions to the duodenum and surrounding tissues. The cyst is periodically decompressed by removing and replacing the cystic duct clip, and dissected laterally, medially, and posteriorly until fully mobilized.

The surgeon carefully dissects the choledochal cyst distally, noting its proximity to the portal vein. Umbilical tape is used for retraction as the cyst is dissected until it tapers sharply near the pancreatic head. The common bile duct is ligated with 3-0 PDS and 3-0 Vicryl sutures and divided after placing a proximal clip.

The surgeon uses traction on the cystic duct stump to manipulate and completely dissect the distal portion of the choledochal cyst from the proximal portal vein towards the porta hepatis. The first and proximal second portions of the duodenum are mobilized. The cyst is divided half a centimeter distal to the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts. A one-centimeter duodenotomy is created five centimeters distal to the pylorus in an anti-mesenteric longitudinal fashion. A hand-sewn interrupted hepatico-duodenostomy is performed using three ovicle sutures, with four stitches for the posterior wall and six stitches for the anterior wall.
The cholecystectomy is completed, and a surgical drain is placed at the anastomotic site.

Post-operative course, the patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the regular nursing floor for recovery. NG tube was removed on post-operative day three. Diet was advanced as tolerated with return of bowel function. She was discharged on post-operative day five after removal of the abdominal drain that was placed. She was doing well five weeks following surgery. Pathology showed type one choledochal cyst with no dysplasia.
